items.text: The collective utilizes intersectional understanding: racism cannot be confronted without acknowledging its intertwinement with colonialism, sexism and class oppression.
items.context: Summary of her political philosophy as articulated through Brixton Black Women's Group organizing; reflected her anticipation of academic intersectionality framework by nearly a decade
items.publicReaction: Recognized retrospectively as precursor to formal intersectionality theory; cited in academic work on history of Black feminist theory and practice
items.source: Synthesized from multiple Black History Month articles and Black Women Radicals database, reflecting how her intersectional approach was documented

items.text: Black women must organize separately and build political power to address issues specific to our experience.
items.context: Foundational rationale for establishing Brixton Black Women's Group in 1973; reflected her analysis that Black women's liberation required autonomous organizing space distinct from both white feminist and male-dominated Black nationalist movements
items.publicReaction: Influenced formation of multiple Black women's groups across UK in 1970s; foundational to development of Black feminist organizing tradition in Britain
items.source: Brixton Black Women's Group formation narrative cited in Wikipedia article on Brixton Black Women's Group, Oxford Dictionary of National Biography, and Black History Month articles

items.text: A roof over your head is not a privilege—it is a right.
items.context: Foundational articulation of her housing justice philosophy; expressed her belief that adequate shelter was fundamental human right, not commodity, shaping her squatting activism
items.publicReaction: Became rallying cry for squatters' rights movements and housing justice advocates; cited as expression of her moral framework in multiple activist histories
items.source: Referenced in Black History Month 2026 article and knowledge4empowerment biography

items.text: We must turn squatting into an art form to ensure homeless families and people can have a roof over their heads.
items.context: Expression of her practical, creative approach to housing activism; describing how systematic squatting campaigns could serve as direct solution to homelessness
items.publicReaction: Described by contemporary activists as turning 'squatting into an art form'; became characterization of her innovative direct action approach to housing justice
items.source: Referenced in multiple biographical accounts describing her squatting activism; cited in Scottish Women's Rights Centre article
